questions: Should test payments send actual dust (e.g. 0.000001 SOL) to validate the wallet is receivable on-chain, or just validate the address format locally? Real dust costs a tx fee (~0.000005 SOL) per grant but gives true chain-level validation.
Is there a preference for how the Discord confirmation flow works — a button/reaction on the thread message, or a DM to the applicant? Currently grants collect the wallet inline in the forum thread.

Should the payment worker support multiple concurrent providers (e.g. future SPL token payouts, USDC on Solana), or is native SOL the only chain target for v1? This affects whether the provider dict is overkill vs. just right.

For the admin retry flow: should retry re-fetch the current SOL price (amount may differ from original) or use the originally quoted price? Re-fetching is safer for the org but the applicant sees a different amount.
Is there an existing Supabase migration workflow (e.g. a migrations directory, a CI step that applies SQL), or is sql/ just reference scripts applied manually?

success_criteria.criterion: payment_requests table exists with all specified columns, constraints, indexes, and RLS policies
claim_due_payment_requests RPC uses FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED and atomically transitions status to processing
After tx submission (status=submitted), ambiguous chain status (not_found) transitions to manual_hold — never auto-retry
Only chain-definitive failure (status check returns 'failed') allows automatic transition to failed state
Private keys never appear in DB columns, log messages, or admin chat tool responses
payment_requests table is NOT in QUERYABLE_TABLES; dedicated query_payments tool redacts wallet addresses
Admin retry_payment only works from failed or manual_hold states — submitted state is blocked
Unique partial index prevents two active (non-failed, non-cancelled) payments for the same producer + producer_ref + is_test combination
On bot restart, recover_inflight correctly resolves processing (no tx_sig → requeue) and submitted (check chain → confirm/fail/hold) states
Grants cog produces payment requests instead of calling solana_client directly; grant status flow updated accordingly
All new unit tests pass (pytest tests/test_payment_service.py)
Full pytest suite passes with no regressions
PaymentProvider ABC is minimal — only send, check_status, get_token_price_usd, token_name methods
Worker loop follows sharing_cog pattern: @tasks.loop, claim batch, sequential process, env-configurable interval and batch size
New files stay under ~300 lines each; payment_service.py may be up to ~400 given the state machine complexity
Test and final payment flows are distinct: is_test flag on payment_requests, test payments can auto-confirm
Discord confirmation uses button interaction (not just message reply) for better UX
Manual staging smoke test: full grant → test-payment → confirm → final-payment → confirmed flow
Manual staging test: kill bot mid-payment, restart, verify recovery

assumptions: v1 targets native SOL transfers only — SPL token support (USDC etc.) is a future extension, but the provider abstraction accommodates it without code changes.
No separate wallet_registry table in v1. Recipient wallets are stored per-payment. A shared wallet registry can be layered on later when multiple producers need it.
The existing SolanaClient class remains unchanged; SolanaProvider is a thin adapter wrapping it.

grant_applications columns (payment_status, wallet_address, tx_signature, sol_amount, sol_price_usd) are left in the table for historical records but new payments flow through payment_requests exclusively.
SQL migrations are applied manually to Supabase — there is no automated migration runner in CI.
The Discord confirmation flow uses the existing forum thread (where the grant lives) rather than DMs — consistent with current UX where wallet submission happens in-thread.
Test payments send real dust (0.000001 SOL) on-chain to validate the wallet, not just local format checks — the cost is trivial and the assurance is high.
The payment worker runs in the same bot process as the sharing worker — no separate deployment.
Admin chat tools follow the existing Anthropic tool-call format used by other tools in tools.py.
